topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed accusingly on my monitor. I’d been cross-referencing departmental budgets, a tedious task, when I landed on Sarah’s compensation package. A slow, cold dread began to pool in my stomach. Her salary, compared to mine, was a noticeable gap. I blinked hard, hoping the numbers were a glitch, a simple transposition error. But the figures held firm. My hands started to tremble slightly as I reread the figures, the air in the cubicle suddenly feeling thin. I forced myself to breathe deeply, focusing on the texture of my mouse, anything to avoid looking directly at the screen again. When Sarah walked by, offering a bright, oblivious smile, I managed a stiff nod, my throat suddenly dry.

[story 2]

It happened during the weekly performance review prep. I was organizing files for the executive summary when I stumbled upon the payroll reports. The realization hit me like a physical blow. The discrepancy in pay between myself and young Mark was substantial. A tightness constricted my chest, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I stared at the screen, my vision momentarily blurring at the edges. I felt a sudden, intense need to physically retreat; I pushed my chair back with a scrape that sounded deafening in the quiet office. When Mark approached, asking about the Q3 projections, my voice came out unexpectedly high and strained. "Just... checking some data," I mumbled, avoiding his eyes entirely.

[story 3]

The office hummed with its usual background noise, but for me, everything had narrowed down to the stark white of the screen displaying the data. I clicked, scrolled, and then stopped. The figures for the junior analyst, Chloe, were significantly higher than what I was earning. A wave of disorientation washed over me, leaving a strange hollowness in my chest. I gripped the edge of my desk, my knuckles turning white. I tried to rationalize it—maybe it was a bonus structure I didn't understand—but the logic felt flimsy, inadequate against the solid reality of the numbers. When Chloe offered me a casual greeting, I managed a strained, overly cheerful tone, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face.
